title: Download FASTQ files from SRA and/or ENA
text:
To download raw FASTQ files from SRA


Browse the files available at NCBI SRA and choose which files to download.
Copy the Accession number for each file:



Copy accession number





Paste the accession number into the European Nucleotide Archive in the top right hand search boxes.



Search for accession number





You want to click both fastq files (e.g. SRR7945367_1.fastq.gz and SRR7945367_2.fastq.gz) to download them.
The file ending _1 is the forward read and the file ending _2 is the reverse read.



Download files





You can now move these files into your working folder - this is the folder on your computer where you are also writing your R and Python code.
Keep a record of which sample is which - I recommend an Excel Spreadsheet with the accession number and which file it is.








Study Accession
Read accession
Sample




SRP162992
SRR7945367
Leishmania braziliensis metacyclic promastigote









Caution



Make sure the accession matches what is on the filename so that you don’t get confused - eg. SRR7945367 for SRR7945367_1.fastq.gz
This is usually the Run Accession
crumbs: Download FASTQ files from SRA and/or ENA

title: Install Hisat2 for mapping
text:
Install Hisat2 for mapping
To install Hisat2
Download the binary from The Hisat2 Github page. For Mac, it is the OSX_x86_64 version.
Double click to decompress the file to give you a folder
Move the folder to somewhere safe in your Documents
Open the folder using the terminal:



Open Terminal at Folder


Check it works
Run
./hisat2 -help
You may need to unblock from Apple security settings (Apple System setting -> Security and Privacy -> Allow Anyway)
Once it works, you want to add this folder to your path (so you can access the program from anywhere)
This command will give you the full folder location:
pwd
For me this was: /Users/lizzie/Documents/Kingston/Bioinformatics_general/hisat2-2.2.1
Then use this folder location (replace with your own) and run the following two lines:
echo 'export PATH="$PATH:/Users/lizzie/Documents/Kingston/Bioinformatics_general/hisat2-2.2.1"' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc






Caution



The >> being doubled is really important here.
>> will take the result and add it to the bottom of the ~/.zshrc file, > would overwrite the file.


You can then open the terminal from a different folder, and you should be able to run:
hisat2 -help
This means the installation has worked.

title: Differential Expression in Python/R
text: Work in Progress - looking at using RNA sequencing data to do differential expression analyses.
